patient_presentation.presenting_complaint: Crushing substernal chest pain for 1 hour, radiating to left arm, with diaphoresis
patient_presentation.history: 58-year-old male, hypertension on lisinopril, ex-smoker (30 pack-years), father died of MI at 60. No prior cardiac workup. Pain began at rest, 9/10 severity, not relieved by rest.

imaging.notes: 12-lead ECG: 3mm ST elevation in V2-V4 with reciprocal depression in II, III, aVF. Portable CXR: clear lung fields, no widened mediastinum. Bedside echo pending.

Hauser.reasoning: Everyone is going to scream STEMI and cath lab. Fine, the ECG and trop scream it too. But before we shovel him toward PCI, I want to make sure we're not missing a Type A aortic dissection presenting with coronary ostial involvement. The hypotension bothers me.

differentials.rationale: ST elevation V2-V4, trop 8.5, classic pain pattern. I hate that it's this obvious.
contradicting_evidence: Borderline hypotension could indicate other etiology

differentials.diagnosis: Type A aortic dissection with RCA/LMCA involvement
differentials.rationale: Hypotensive male with chest pain. Dissection can mimic MI when it shears a coronary ostium. CXR is reassuring but not definitive.
Lower on list but ST changes can mimic.
contradicting_evidence: Trop too high for simple pericarditis

proposed_tests.test_name: Bedside TTE with aortic root view
proposed_tests.rationale: Rule out dissection flap and assess wall motion before cath
rules_out: Type A dissection if root clean

proposed_tests.rationale: Definitive for STEMI, time is muscle

challenges.content: Forman, before you push him to the cath lab on autopilot, tell me you've actually considered why he's hypotensive. Cardiogenic shock vs dissection vs RV involvement — which is it?

Forman.reasoning: Pretest probability of STEMI here is overwhelming: 58M, classic pain, ECG criteria met, trop 8.5. Posterior probability after that ECG is north of 0.9. Hauser's dissection hunt has a base rate of ~3 per 100k; we shouldn't delay reperfusion for a zebra.

differentials.rationale: Meets STEMI criteria with biomarker confirmation. Anterior distribution suggests proximal LAD. Hypotension likely early cardiogenic shock or RV involvement.
supporting_evidence: Risk factors: HTN, smoking, family hx

differentials.diagnosis: NSTEMI with early ST changes / Wellens evolution
differentials.rationale: Mostly academic given STE criteria already met.
Pretest low, no widened mediastinum, no tearing pain.

proposed_tests.test_name: Activate cath lab for primary PCI
proposed_tests.rationale: Door-to-balloon <90 min is the standard. Every 30 min of delay = ~7.5% mortality increase.
Cheap, fast — RV infarct changes management (avoid nitrates, give fluids).

challenges.content: Hauser, dissection prevalence here is well under 1%. The CXR is clean and the ECG is diagnostic. We do not delay PCI to chase a zebra unless we get a hard signal.

Carmen.reasoning: No autoimmune signal here. No rash, no arthralgias, no serositis history. Vasculitis with coronary involvement is on the shelf but it's a stretch in a 58M with classic risk factors.

differentials.rationale: Agree with cardiology read. No autoimmune contribution apparent.

Chen.reasoning: This is a time-critical surgical-adjacent emergency. Cath lab now. If the angio shows triple vessel or a left main, we're calling CT surgery for CABG. Door-to-balloon clock is running.

differentials.rationale: Clinical, ECG, and biomarker criteria all met. Hypotension means we may be looking at cardiogenic shock — consider IABP or Impella support.

differentials.diagnosis: Mechanical complication (early papillary muscle rupture, free wall)
differentials.rationale: Too early in course but watch for it post-reperfusion.

proposed_tests.test_name: Bedside echo for EF and mechanical complications
proposed_tests.rationale: Guides need for mechanical circulatory support

challenges.content: Hauser, we can do a 30-second aortic root look on bedside echo en route to the lab. We are not delaying reperfusion.

Wills.reasoning: No oncologic angle in an acute STEMI. No constitutional symptoms, no lymphadenopathy, no concerning imaging findings. Defer to cardiology and surgical teams.

Hauser.reasoning: Bedside echo shows anterior wall hypokinesis, aortic root is clean, no flap. Trop trending up to 12.4. Fine. It's a STEMI. I'll concede the dissection. But somebody owes me a follow-up CT chest after PCI just to be sure.

differentials.rationale: Echo confirms regional WMA in LAD distribution, root clean, troponin trajectory consistent with infarct.
Conceded. Root looks clean on bedside study.

proposed_tests.rationale: Definitively rule out dissection once stable. Cheap insurance.

Chen.reasoning: Patient is in the cath lab. 100% proximal LAD occlusion confirmed, drug-eluting stent placed, TIMI-3 flow restored. EF 35%. Hemodynamics improved post-reperfusion. This is settled.

differentials.diagnosis: Anterior STEMI s/p successful PCI to proximal LAD
differentials.rationale: Angiographically confirmed culprit lesion, successfully revascularized.

proposed_tests.test_name: Dual antiplatelet therapy + high-intensity statin + beta-blocker as tolerated
